segments.text: Now, we are at the Book of the Tens, Angutra Nikaya, Sutta No. 46.
Once the Exalted One was staying among the Sakyans at Kapilavatu in Banyan Park.
At that time, a number of Sakyan lay followers, that day being the Uposatha, came to visit
the Exalted One, and on coming to Him, saluted Him and sat down at one side.
So seated, the Exalted One said this to those Sakyan lay followers.
Sakyans, are you keeping the Uposatha with the eight precepts?
Stop here for a moment to make some comments.
This Uposatha, they translate as the Sabbath, the day when the lay people are supposed to keep
the eight precepts.
And this was a tradition in India, even before the Buddha's time.
That is why the Buddha asked his lay disciples to follow.
This tradition of keeping the Uposatha, it's on the eighth day of the lunar calendar, the
15th, the 23rd, and the 30th, or 29th, which means approximately once a week.
And from there, you can see other religions like the Christians, they also keep the Sabbath.
But for the Christians, it's the Sunday.
And for the Buddhists, they keep the eight precepts.
The eight precepts, the first precept is not to kill, the second is not to take what is
not given, the third is not to engage in sexual conduct, the fourth is not to lie, the fifth
is not to take intoxicants, the sixth is not to eat afternoon, the period between noon
and the next morning dawn.
The seventh precept is not to adorn the body, and also not to watch shows, not to enjoy
music, etc.
And the eighth precept is not to sleep on high and wide or luxurious beds.
These are the eight precepts.
Now to continue with the sutta.
So the Sarkans replied,
Sometimes sir, we do so, but maybe sometimes we don't.
And the Buddha said, Well sarkans, in those matters, it is no gain to you, it is ill-gotten
by you, since in a life does conjoin to fear of grief, in a life does conjoin to fear of
death, you sometimes do and sometimes maybe do not keep the opusata with the eight precepts.
But thank you sarkans, suppose a man here, without meeting an unlucky day, were to earn
half a kahapana in some business or other, might not folk well say of him, a clever fellow
full of energy, stop here for a moment to comment, this kahapana is a measure of money
used in the Buddha's days.
Then they replied, Yes sir, they might.
And the Buddha said, Well thank you sarkans, suppose a man here, without meeting an unlucky
day, were to earn a kahapana or two or more, up to fifty kahapanas in some business or other,
might not folk well say of him, a clever fellow full of energy, yes sir they might.
Now what do you think sarkans, suppose that man day by day earning a hundred or a thousand
kahapanas and hoarding up what he got, were to reach a hundred years, were to live a hundred
years, would not he amass great wealth, he would sir.
Well sarkans, what thank you, would that man, because of his wealth, on account of his wealth,
in consequence of his wealth, would that man, for a single night or a single day, or even
half a night or half a day, live in the enjoyment of utter happiness, surely not sir.
And why not, because sir, sensual pleasures are impermanent, hollow, false, of the nature
of deception.
I stop here for a moment, here utter happiness seems to refer to the heavenly happiness or
the bliss of jhana, meditative absorption.
And in other sutras, the Buddha has said that, but maybe here it refers more to sensual pleasures,
here it is said that sensual pleasures are impermanent, hollow, false, of the nature
of deception.
And in some other sutras, the Buddha has said that sensual pleasures can be likened to a
bone without any meat thrown to a hungry dog.
And the dog is very hungry and you throw a piece of bone, a big piece of bone, you will
bite at the bone, not at the bone, but the hunger cannot be satisfied.
So it is the same with sensual pleasures, it just gives us a fleeting happiness, but
it is very impermanent and so that is why it is said that it is hollow and false of
the nature of deception.
Now the Buddha has said, now sarkyans, suppose here a follower of mine, living serious, ardent
and resolved, were to live faring onward as I have advised for ten years, he would spend
a hundred years, a hundred times a hundred years, a hundred times a thousand years, a
hundred times a hundred thousand years enjoying utter happiness and he would be a once-returner,
or a non-returner, or a winner of surety, a stream winner, stop here for a moment.
In the heavens, the lifespan is in terms of millions of years, that is why here the Buddha
said such a long time this person can enjoy.
Once-returner is Sakkadegamin, is a person who has attained the second fruit of Aryahutna,
and a non-returner is an Anagami, the third fruit of Aryahutna, and a stream winner is
a Tussotapanna, the first fruit of Arya.
Then the Buddha continued, that alone ten years sarkyans, suppose here a follower of
mine were to live faring onward as I have advised for nine, eight, seven, six, or even
for a single year, he would live for a hundred years, up to a hundred times a hundred thousand
years enjoying utter happiness, and he would be a once-returner, or a non-returner, or
at any rate a stream winner, let alone a single year sarkyans.
Suppose here a follower of mine living serious, ardent, and resolved were to live faring onward
as I have advised for ten months, for nine, eight, up to a single month, even half a month,
even ten days and nights, for seven, six, five, even if he were to live this for a single
night and day, he would spend a hundred years, a hundred times a hundred years, a hundred
times a thousand years, a hundred times a hundred thousand years enjoying utter happiness,
and he would be a once-returner, or a non-returner, or a winner of charity, a stream winner.
In these matters sarkyans, it is no gain to you, this ill-gotten by you, since in a life
thus conjoined to fear of grief, in a life thus conjoined to fear of death, you sometimes
do and sometimes do not keep the upasata with the eight precepts.
Then they said, then sir, from this day forth, we here will observe the upasata with the
eight precepts.
So you see, the Buddha is saying that if you keep the eight precepts constantly on the
upasata days, that means approximately once a week, then the merit is very great, that
will lead you to a rebirth in heaven.
And life is very uncertain, we don't know when we are going to pass away.
Just recently, about a week ago, a former copier of mine, Peng Lim, just forty-nine
years old, passed away.
Those of you who have been following me for the last ten years or so would know him.
So you see, he is even younger than me, you don't expect him to pass away, and suddenly
he just passed away.
So life is so uncertain, we don't know when we are going to pass away.
So we should always be prepared, as the scout motor says.
So turn number forty-seven.
Once the exalted one was staying near Vesali in Great Wood, at the house of the peak roof,
then Mahali, the Lichavite, came to see the exalted one and said this to him,
Sir, what is the reason, what is the cause of doing an evil deed, of committing an evil
deed?
And the Buddha said, greed, Mahali, is the reason.
Greed is the cause of doing an evil deed, of committing an evil deed.
Hatred, Mahali, is the reason and cause.
Delusion is the reason and cause, not having other consideration.
Wrongly directed thought, these are the reasons, these are the causes of doing an evil deed,
of committing an evil deed.
I just stopped here for a moment to comment.
These five things, five causes of doing evil, the first one is greed, Pali word is loba.
The second is hatred, dosa.
The third is delusion, moha.
The fourth is not having other consideration, ah yoniso manasikara.
That means not having the ability to consider properly.
The fifth one is wrongly directed thought, micha panihitang, wrongly directed thought.
These are the causes, and then they asked, sir, what is the reason, what is the cause
of doing a good deed, of committing a lovely deed?
And the Buddha replied, non-greed, Mahali, is the reason.
Non-greed is the cause, non-hatred, non-delusion, terror consideration, rightly directed thought,
Mahali, is the reason, the cause of doing a good deed, of committing a lovely deed.
These are the reasons and the causes of it.
Muroga Mahali, if these ten dhammas or qualities were not found existing in the world, there
would be here no proclaiming of wrong living and crooked living, or of right living and
straight living.
But since Mahali, these ten qualities are found existing in the world, therefore is there
proclaiming of wrong and crooked living, of right and straight living, that's the end of
the sutta.
Here these ten qualities probably refers to these five causes of doing an evil deed and
the five causes of doing a good deed, what that means, greed, hatred, delusion, not having
terror consideration, wrongly directed thought, and then the converse, the other five is non-greed,
non-hatred, non-delusion, having terror consideration, rightly directed thoughts.
So five plus five is these ten dhammas, the first five contributes to wrong living and
then the second five contributes to right living.
So greed you all understand what it is, hatred also, delusion is understanding the nature
of things, not being able to see clearly, because most of us are enveloped by the five
hindrances that cover us and do not enable us to see things as they really are.
And then terror consideration, uniso manasikara means able to contemplate a problem up to
the source of it, up to the root of the problem.
So not having terror consideration is not having the ability to consider a problem thoroughly.
Wrongly directed thought is the thought that is directed in wrong ways.
For example, having sensual desire in the thoughts or hatred, this hatred, malice, and
then hinsa, harmful, harmfulness, the wrong thoughts.
To the next sutta, number forty-eight, the Buddha said, monks, these ten conditions must
again and again be contemplated by one who has gone forth from the home life, what ten?
He must again and again contemplate these facts, one, I am now come to a state of being an
outcast, number two, my very life is dependent on others, number three, I must now conduct
myself differently, number four, do I reproach myself as to my virtues, number five, would
my wise companions in the holy life, after examining me, find me reproachable as to my
virtues, number six, all that is mine, beloved and pleasing, will become otherwise, will
become separated from me, number seven, I am the owner of my kamma, heir to my kamma,
born of my kamma, related to my kamma, abide supported by my kamma, whatever kamma I shall
do for good or for ill, of that I shall be the heir, number eight, how do I spend my
days and nights that are relentlessly passing by, number nine, do I take delight in a secluded
dwelling, number ten, have I any supernormal attainment, any arian vision and knowledge,
whereby when questioned by my companions in the holy life, I shall not be confounded,
these monks are the ten conditions to be again and again contemplated by one who has gone
forth from the home life, at the end of the sutta, the sutta is principally for monks
and these are very important contemplations a monk has to make, first one he has to remember
that he is now an outcast, an outcast from society, he has dissociated himself from society
and because he has no status in the sense that he has no money, has no position in the world,
so most people in the world will also not want to associate with him, so he has become an outcast,
which suits him ideally if he wants to practice and cultivate the holy life, then number two,
my very life is dependent on others, this is a fact for a monk which is quite frightening for
a normal lay person, that's why it's not easy for a lay person to become a monk,
number three I must now conduct myself differently etc, so the others I shall not
bother to explain, it is small for monks to contemplate, now the sutta number 50,
once the exalted one was staying near Savati at Jeter Grove in Anathapindika Park,
on that occasion a number of monks, after returning from their arms round and eating their meal,
as they sat assembled together in the service hall, remained in strife and uproar and dispute,
abusing each other with the weapons of the tongue, now the exalted one in the evening,
rising from his seclusion approached the service hall and on reaching it sat down on a seat made
ready, when he had sat down the exalted one addressed the monk saying, monks in what talk were
you engaged as you sat here and what was the topic of your talk left unfinished, and they said here
sir after returning from our arms round and eating our meal, we sat assembled together in the service
hall and remained in strife, uproar and dispute, abusing each other with the weapons of the tongue,
and the Buddha said, monks it is not seemly for you clansmen who in faith went forth from the home to
the homeless life, thus to abide in strife, uproar and dispute, abusing each other with the weapons
of the tongue, monks there are these ten conditions to be remembered, which as they make for affection
and respect, do conduce to fellow feeling, to not quarreling, to concord and unity, what ten,
herein a monk is virtuous, restrained with the restraint of the patimokha, proficient in following
the practice of right conduct, seeing ground for fear in the minutest faults, and takes upon
and trains himself in the rules of morality, insofar as a monk is virtuous and so far,
and so trains himself, this is the condition to be remembered, which as it makes for affection
and respect, does conduce to fellow feeling, to not quarreling, to concord and unity,
number two, then again a monk has heard much, he bears in mind what he has heard,
he hoards up what he has heard, those teachings which are lovely in the beginning, middle and end,
proclaim in spirit and in letter, they all fulfilled utterly purified holy life, such like
are the teachings he has heard much, born in mind, practiced in speech, pondered in the heart,
and rightly penetrated by view, insofar as he has heard much, etc., this is a condition to be
remembered, which conduces to concord and unity, number three, again a monk has a good friend,
a good companion, a good intimate, insofar as he is such, this is a condition to be remembered,
number four, again a monk is easy to speak to, possessed of qualities which make him easy to
speak to, he is tractable, capable of being instructed, insofar as he is such, this is a
condition to be remembered, number five, in all the undertakings of his fellows in the holy life,
be they matters weighty or trivial, he is shrewd and energetic, having ability to give proper
consideration, therefore as to what is the right thing to do and how to manage it, insofar as a
monk is such, this is a condition to be remembered, which conduces to unity and concord, number six,
again a monk delights in dhamma, is pleasant to converse with, rejoices exceedingly in higher dhamma,
in higher vinaya, insofar as a monk is such, this is a condition to be remembered, number seven,
again a monk dwells resolute in energy for the abandoning of bad qualities, stout and strong
to acquire good qualities, insofar as a monk is such, this is a condition to be remembered,
number eight, then again a monk is content with whatsoever supply of robes, arms, food, bed and
lodging, comforts and necessaries in sickness he may get, insofar as a monk is such, this is a
condition to be remembered, number nine, again a monk is collected, possessed of mindful discrimination
in the highest degree, able to call to mind and remember a thing done and said long ago, insofar
as a monk is such, this is a condition to be remembered, number ten, lastly a monk is possessed
of wisdom, he has wisdom for tracing out the arising and seizing of things, wisdom which is
aryan, penetrating, going on to the utter destruction of ill, insofar as a monk has such
wisdom, this also is a condition to be remembered, which as it makes for affection and respect,
does conduce to fellow feeling, to not quarreling, to concord and unity, these monks are the ten
conditions to be remembered, which conduce to concord and unity, as the end of the sutta, so this
is another sutta, more for monks and also another very practical sutta, where a monk is encouraged
to cultivate these ten qualities, so because they conduce to concord and unity, harmony in the
sangha, the first one the monk should be virtuous, he should be restrained by the restraint of the
patimokha, patimokha is the code of precepts, contains 227 precepts of a Theravada monk,
so a monk is supposed to abide by these precepts as much as he can, number two, a monk should have
much knowledge of the dhamma, and he should understand the dhamma, meaning the suttas,
the discourses of the Buddha, he should ponder in his mind and understand these suttas,
this is the second thing that conduces to harmony and unity of the sangha,
number three, the monk should have a good friend, here a good friend is a Kalyana Mitta,
in Mahayana books they translate it as a good knowing advisor, he is supposed to be a teacher,
a fellow monk who can guide a monk in the dhamma vinaya, in the holy life, number four,
a monk should be easy to speak to, not stubborn, in other words he should be willing to listen to
instructions, to listen to advice and carry them out, that means he is willing to listen,
number five, he should share in all the duties and work of the sangha in the monastery and do his
best to carry out all the duties, whether it's a heavy duty or a light duty as well as he can,
this also conduces to harmony and unity of the sangha, number six, a monk should delight in dhamma,
in the higher dhamma and the higher vinaya, higher dhamma, abhidhamma, here refers to the higher
teachings in the dhamma, not the later seven books because the later set of seven books of abhidhamma
definitely was written much later, so here in the in the sutras normally when the Buddha talks
about the higher dhamma, he usually refers to those dhammas that you cultivate to get out of
samsara, usually the Buddha mentions a set of 37 requisites of enlightenment, that's Pali's body
pakya dhammas and these 37 requisites of enlightenment is also equivalent to the noble eightfold path
and here normally when the Buddha talks about the higher dhamma, he also talks about the higher
vinaya, the higher monastic discipline, there is no set of books called abhidhamma, this says during
the Buddha's time there was no set of teachings called abhidhamma, then number seven, a monk should
be resolute in energy, should have made a lot of effort to practice the holy life, to abandon his
bad qualities and to acquire good qualities, bad qualities are like greed, hatred, delusion,
sensual desire, ill will, sloth and topper, restlessness, doubt, these are the five hindrances,
jealousy, arrogance etc. Number eight, the monk should be content with whatever supply of requisites
that he receives, food, robes, bed and lodging, medicines etc. Contentment comes from within
is a quality, not dependent so much on outside things, it's a quality of mind that has to be
cultivated. Then number nine, here he's talking about sati, sati here, recollection, the definition
is given here as in some other sutras, is able to call to mind and remember a thing done and said
long ago, so sati is the quality of remembering and when applied to the practice of the holy life,
it means to remember what you have to practice, it means to remember your object of meditation,
to remember that life is anicha, impermanent, life is dukkha, suffering and life is anatta,
there is no self etc. So this sati is a quality of remembering whatever you want to practice.
So number 10 is a quality of wisdom, wisdom to be able to see the arising and seizing of
everything in the world, everything that arises must cease because if you see the impermanent
nature of the world, then you can become very disenchanted with everything in the world.
